---
title: Predicting Body Fat Percentage
author:
  - name: Clea Yenifer (01477943)
  - name: Sutolimin Widjaja (01540923)
  - name: Vincent Lavoie (01550327)
  - name: Yuchi Yu (01549686)
address:
    address: Singapore Management University
lead_author_surname: Group 2
abstract: |
  This study investigates whether body fat percentage, a key indicator of health, can be accurately predicted using simple and accessible body measurements. Using a dataset of physical characteristics, a multiple linear regression model was developed to identify the most relevant predictors. Feature selection methods were then applied, and candidate models were evaluated based on out-of-sample performance. The final model, consisting of age, abdomen circumference, wrist circumference, and height, achieved the best balance between predictive accuracy and simplicity. It explains approximately 73% of the variation in body fat percentage. Overall, the findings suggest that body fat percentage can be reliably estimated using a small set of easily obtainable measurements, offering a simple and practical predictive tool.
papersize: letter
fontsize: 9pt
# Optional: Force one-column layout, default is two-column
#one_column: true
# Optional: Enables lineno mode, but only if one_column mode is also true
#lineno: true
# Optional: Enable one-sided layout, default is two-sided
#one_sided: true
# Optional: Enable section numbering, default is unnumbered
numbersections: true
# Optional: Specify the depth of section number, default is 5
#secnumdepth: 5
# Optional: Skip inserting final break between acknowledgements, default is false
skip_final_break: true
footer_contents: "Thinking with Data Group Project"
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \bibliographystyle{jss}
  - \captionsetup[figure]{justification=centering,singlelinecheck=false}
output: pinp::pinp
natbib: true
biblio-title: "References"
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{YourPackage-vignetteentry}
  %\VignetteKeywords{YourPackage, r, anotherkeyword}
  %\VignettePackage{YourPackage}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# Introduction

Body fat percentage is a key indicator of health that provides more
meaningful information than body weight alone. It is commonly used to
assess fitness levels and health risks, but accurate measurement
typically requires clinical methods such as DEXA scans, which are often
costly and not easily accessible.

This study aims to determine whether body fat percentage can be
accurately predicted using simple and easily measurable body
characteristics. Specifically, it addresses the following questions: how
body fat percentage is related to basic body measurement variables;
which variables are the most important predictors of body fat
percentage; and whether a parsimonious model can achieve strong
predictive performance compared to more complex alternatives. These
questions are important in assessing whether simple body measurements
can serve as a practical and reliable alternative to more complex and
costly measurement methods.

To investigate this, we use a dataset of physical characteristics as a
training set and evaluate multiple linear regression models to determine
which specification provides the best balance of predictive performance
and simplicity.

# Data Set

The bodyfat dataset contains 250 observations of men of various ages.
The raw dataset consists of 16 variables, with body fat percentage
($pct.bf$) as the dependent variable. Aside from density, pct.bf, age,
weight, and height, the remaining variables are body circumferences (in
centimetres) measuring the perimeter around different body parts. A full
summary of the variables is provided in Figure \ref{fig:corr} in the
appendix.

## Exploratory Data Analysis

From the correlation matrix plot (Figure \ref{fig:corr}), we observe a
near-perfect negative correlation (-0.99) between density and body fat
percentage. This means we can predict body fat percentage solely from
density. However, we note that density is calculated by dividing body
mass (weight) by body volume. Although weight is easy to obtain,
measuring body volume requires specialised methods such as water
displacement, which are costly and not easily accessible. Hence, we
decided to drop density from our dataset. (Figure \ref{fig:corr}) also
shows a perfect positive correlation between waist and abdomen,
indicating that these variables are effectively interchangeable. So we
removed waist and retained abdomen.

# Analysis

## Data Transformation

Height was recorded in inches while all other body measurements (chest,
abdomen, and so on) were in centimeters, so height was converted to
centimeters for consistency. Weight was converted from pounds to
kilograms to align with standard metric units commonly used. We observe
that the dependent variable, body fat percentage, has one observation
with value 0, which we omitted and identified as measurement error since
this is not scientifically possible \citep{menshealth2020}.

## Initial Model Experimentation

We start by fitting a linear model using all predictors except waist.
The residuals vs fitted plot (Figure \ref{fig:residplot}) shows a mild
wave-like pattern showing slight overestimation at the lower and upper
ends. However, the deviations are small and likely driven by sparse data
(at upper end) rather than genuine nonlinearity. The linearity
assumption is considered reasonably satisfied.

The model achieves an $R^2$ value of 0.7466 ($Adjusted\ R^2$= 0.7326),
though only age, abdomen, and wrist are statistically significant. Given
the strong correlations between predictor variables observed in EDA, we
check for multicollinearity using VIF. Weight stands out with a VIF over
40, while being heavily correlated with other predictors (see Figure
\ref{fig:corr}). Furthermore, weight does not distinguish between fat
and lean mass, so its inclusion may inflate model fit without adding
meaningful predictive value. Weight is thus excluded.

Refitting without weight keeps $R^2$ unchanged but raises
$Adjusted\ R^2$ to 0.734. Chest, abdomen, hip, and thigh still show VIF
\> 5, as they measure overlapping body parts. We retain abdomen over
chest and hip over thigh based on stronger correlations with body fat.
So chest and thigh are dropped, confirmed by their high p-values. Upon
refitting, hip continues to show high VIF with abdomen and remains
insignificant, so it is dropped too.

Knee and ankle are removed at this stage. Intuitively, these are joints
whose size reflects bone structure rather than fat accumulation,
confirmed by their high p-values. Bicep and forearm are unreliable fat
predictors as someone with large biceps could have either low (muscular)
or high (larger person) body fat, confirmed by their high p-values.
Neck, while borderline significant (p = 0.058), becomes insignificant
once bicep and forearm are removed, suggesting its earlier significance
was partly driven by surrounding noise. All three are dropped, giving
our first candidate model (age + abdomen + wrist + height_cm). An ANOVA
test comparing the full model against the reduced model confirms that
the dropped predictors do not meaningfully improve fit (F = 1.43, p =
0.185).

## Model Comparison

After manually arriving at a candidate model, we also identified
candidate models through three feature selection approaches:

### Exhaustive Search

We first perform an exhaustive search, which considers all possible
combinations of predictors. Each model is evaluated using BIC (Bayesian
Information Criterion), which, compared to the Akaike Information
Criterion, imposes a heavier penalty on model complexity and therefore
tends to favour simpler, more parsimonious models. As this aligns with
our objective, we adopt BIC as our selection criterion.

Based on Figure \ref{fig:exh}, we observe that BIC reaches its minimum
when the model includes 4 predictors. Therefore, we select the model
with age, height, abdomen, and wrist (Figure \ref{fig:exh2}).
Interestingly, we obtain the same model as the first candidate model.

### Backward Variable Selection

We also conducted backward variable selection based on BIC. In this
approach, variables are removed one at a time until the model reaches
its minimum BIC value. Based on Figure \ref{fig:backward}, the model
achieves the lowest BIC when age, height, abdomen, and wrist are
included as predictors. Notably, this also results in the same set of
predictors as those obtained from the manual and exhaustive search
methods.

### Forward Variable Selection

For further comparison, we also performed forward variable selection
based on BIC. In this approach, variables are added one at a time until
the model reaches its minimum BIC value. According to Figure
\ref{fig:forward}, the resulting model includes abdomen, weight, and
wrist as predictors, which becomes our second candidate model.

To assess which candidate model is best, we perform out-of-sample tests
next.

## Cross Validation

To evaluate the out-of-sample performance on unseen data, we apply
10-fold cross-validation across our 2 candidate models. This repeatedly partitions
the data into 10 equal folds, training on 9 and testing on the remaining
1, cycling through all folds.

We tested this approach on 2 different models:

-   Model 1: Manual/Exhaustive search/Backward selection

\vspace{-12pt}

\begin{multline}
\widehat{pct.bf} = 3.69 + 0.056\,age + 0.767\,abdomen \\ 
- 1.929\,wrist - 0.128\,height\_cm
\label{eqn:model1}
\end{multline} \vspace{-12pt}

-   Model 2: Forward variable selection

\vspace{-12pt}

\begin{multline}
\widehat{pct.bf} = -27.259 + 0.957\,abdomen \\ 
- 0.208\,weight - 1.388\,wrist
\label{eqn:model2}
\end{multline} \vspace{-12pt}

Both models perform similarly in terms of predictive ability. Based on MAE and RMSE (see Figure \ref{fig:crossval}), Model 1 performs
slightly better than Model 2, with MAE lower by 0.034 and RMSE lower by
0.031. This suggests Model 1 produces more accurate predictions on
average and is slightly better at limiting large prediction errors.

In contrast, Model 2 has a marginally higher $R^2$ (0.745 vs 0.724),
indicating that it explains a slightly greater proportion of the
variation in body fat percentage. However, a higher $R^2$ does not
necessarily imply better predictive performance, especially in this
case. The improvement in $R^2$ is likely driven by the inclusion of
weight, which we identified as a problematic variable due to
multicollinearity and weak relevance.

Importantly, both exhaustive and backward selection procedures (based on
BIC) converge to the same specification as Model 1. This provides
additional evidence that a parsimonious specification can achieve
comparable, if not better, predictive performance. Therefore, Model 1 is
preferred as the more parsimonious and robust model, retaining only the
most reliable predictors while avoiding unnecessary complexity.

## Assumptions Checking

In Figure \ref{fig:residplot2}, the residuals vs fitted values plot for
the final model shows a mild wave-like pattern, similar to Figure
\ref{fig:residplot}. However, as discussed previously, this is likely
driven by limited observations at the upper extremes rather than true
non-linearity, so the linearity assumption is considered satisfied. The
Q-Q plot is approximately linear with no major violations of normality,
and the scale-location plot shows a roughly horizontal trend with no
clear evidence of heteroskedasticity (Figure \ref{fig:scaleloc}).
Independence is assumed as each observation corresponds to a different
individual with no repeated measurements.

Finally, no observations exceed three standard deviations from the mean,
suggesting that there are no significant outliers.

# Discussion and Conclusion

The final model achieved an $R^2$ value of 0.73, meaning that 73% of the
variation in body fat percentage is explained by the model.

Abdomen is the strongest predictor, with a coefficient of 0.77. This means a 1 cm increase in abdomen circumference is associated with a 0.77 increase in body fat percentage, holding other variables constant. Age showed a positive association, since people become less active and their bodies change composition as they get older. Height has a negative association, likely because taller individuals distribute mass over a larger frame, resulting in lower body fat.

Wrist circumference had a negative coefficient (-1.93), suggesting that larger wrists are associated with lower body fat. Interestingly, we observed that wrist showed a slight positive
correlation with body fat in isolation, but reverses direction in the
full model - an example of Simpson's paradox. Future work should
investigate this further by exploring alternative specifications
and additional predictors to better understand the true role of wrist
circumference in predicting body fat percentage.

The 95% prediction interval plot (Figure \ref{fig:pred_interval}) shows predictions closely follow the diagonal, indicating good overall fit. However, the interval is relatively wide and dips into negative values at the lower end, which is not physically possible. This suggests reduced reliability for individuals with very low body fat. Future work could explore bounded models such as beta regression.

Key limitations include: First, the sample dataset
includes only adult males, so model results may not generalise well to other populations. Second, while the sample size is adequate, it may be insufficient to reliably capture patterns at
the extremes of the distribution. Finally, the predictor variables rely
on manual tape measurements, potentially introducing slight measurement error.

Future research could address these by including more
diverse populations, larger datasets, and additional
predictors such as physical activity and diet. Overall, the model
shows that body fat percentage can be reasonably estimated
from a small set of body measurements, with relatively good predictive power.

\vspace{-12pt}

\begin{multline}
\widehat{pct.bf} = 3.69 + 0.056\,age + 0.767\,abdomen \\ 
- 1.929\,wrist - 0.128\,height\_cm
\label{eqn:model1}
\end{multline} \vspace{-12pt}

\bibliography{pinp}

\clearpage
\appendix
\section{Appendix}
\begin{center}
\begin{minipage}{\textwidth}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth, height=7in]{summarytable.png}
\captionof{figure}{Table Summary of Dataset}
\label{fig:summary}
\end{minipage}
\end{center}

```{r figure2, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='hide'}
bodyfat <- read.table("../bodyfat.txt", header = TRUE, sep = "\t", check.names = TRUE)

invisible(pdf("figure2.pdf", width = 6, height = 5))

corrplot::corrplot.mixed(cor(bodyfat), upper="ellipse", lower="number", 
                         tl.pos="lt", tl.cex = 0.6, number.cex = 0.6)

dev.off()
```

\begin{figure*}[h]
  \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth, height=4in, page =1]{figure2}
  \end{center}
  \caption{Correlation Matrix Plot\label{fig:corr}}
\end{figure*}
\begin{figure*}[h]
  \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth, height=3.5in]{residplot_1.png}
  \end{center}
  \caption{Residual vs Fitted Plot\label{fig:residplot}}
\end{figure*}
\begin{figure*}[h]
  \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth, height=3.3in]{exhaustive.png}
  \end{center}
  \caption{Exhaustive Search\label{fig:exh}}
\end{figure*}
\begin{figure*}[h]
  \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth, height=2.7in]{exhaustive2.png}
  \end{center}
  \caption{Exhaustive Search (Variables Selection)\label{fig:exh2}}
\end{figure*}
\begin{figure*}[h]
  \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth, height=1.7in]{backward_bic.png}
  \end{center}
  \caption{Backward Variable Selection\label{fig:backward}}
\end{figure*}
\begin{figure*}[h]
  \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth, height=1.7in]{forward_bic.png}
  \end{center}
  \caption{Forward Variable Selection\label{fig:forward}}
\end{figure*}
\begin{figure*}[h]
  \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth, height=2in]{crossval.png}
  \end{center}
  \caption{Cross validation results to compare candidate model performance\label{fig:crossval}}
\end{figure*}
\begin{figure*}[h]
  \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth, height=3.25in]{residplot_2.png}
  \end{center}
  \caption{Side-by-side diagnostic plots: residuals vs fitted values and normal Q-Q plot for final model\label{fig:residplot2}}
\end{figure*}
\begin{figure*}[h]
  \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth, height=3.5in]{scaleloc.png}
  \end{center}
  \caption{Scale Location Plot to assess heteroskedasticity\label{fig:scaleloc}}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure*}[h]
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth, height=4in]{prediction_interval.png}
\end{center}
\caption{95\% Prediction Interval\label{fig:pred_interval}}
\end{figure*}
